Description: Always give a code example or concrete scenario BEFORE explaining the theory. Never start with abstract definitions.
Example_Good: First show: ```cuda __global__ void add(int *a, int *b, int *c) { ... }``` Then explain: 'A CUDA kernel is a function that runs on the GPU...'
Example_Bad: 'A CUDA kernel is a function declared with __global__ that executes on the GPU device. It is called from host code...' (Theory first, boring)

Description: Structure every concept explanation as: 1) Why do we need this? (pain point) 2) What is it? (definition + example) 3) How does it work? (step-by-step + diagram)
Example_Good: ## Why? Accessing global memory is 100x slower than shared memory. ## What? Shared memory is fast on-chip storage. ## How? Declare with __shared__, load tile, sync, compute.
Example_Bad: Just dumping the definition without motivation or usage steps.
When_To_Apply: Every concept section in lecture notes and atomic notes

Description: Each atomic concept note covers exactly ONE idea. If you need to explain two related but distinct concepts, create two separate notes linked with [[wiki-links]].
Example_Good: Separate files: 'Shared-Memory.md' and 'Bank-Conflicts.md' linked via [[Bank Conflicts]]
Example_Bad: One giant file covering shared memory AND bank conflicts AND tiling all together

Description: All technical terms use format: English Term（中文术语）with English as primary. First occurrence in a section should be bilingual; subsequent uses can be English-only.
Example_Good: Shared Memory（共享内存）is fast on-chip storage. Access to Shared Memory requires synchronization.
Example_Bad: 共享内存 is fast on-chip storage. (Chinese-first) OR Shared Memory is fast on-chip storage. (No Chinese at all)
When_To_Apply: Every technical term on first appearance

Description: When explaining two or more similar/contrasting concepts, ALWAYS use a comparison table. Tables are the fastest way to show differences.
Example_Good: | Feature | AoS | SoA | |---|---|---| | Memory Pattern | Scattered | Coalesced | | Cache Friendly | No | Yes |
Example_Bad: 'AoS stores data as an array of structures while SoA stores each field separately...' (Wall of text comparing two things)
When_To_Apply: Whenever comparing 2+ alternatives, approaches, or concepts

Description: NEVER use ASCII art boxes or text diagrams. Always use Mermaid. Obsidian renders Mermaid natively.
Example_Good: ```mermaid graph TB A[Registers] --> B[Shared Memory] --> C[Global Memory] ```
When_To_Apply: Any diagram, flowchart, or visual representation

Description: Every core concept in a lecture note MUST have at least one Mermaid diagram OR comparison table. No concept should be text-only.
Example_Good: Section on Memory Hierarchy includes a graph TB showing the layers with latency annotations
Example_Bad: Section on Memory Hierarchy with only paragraphs of text, no visual aid

Description: Code examples must have inline comments explaining non-obvious lines. Use '// ← explanation' format for key lines.
Example_Good: ```cuda __shared__ float tile[N][N]; // ← Declare shared memory for the tile tile[ty][tx] = A[row*N + tx]; // ← Load from global to shared __syncthreads(); // ← Wait for all threads to finish loading ```
Example_Bad: ```cuda __shared__ float tile[N][N]; tile[ty][tx] = A[row*N + tx]; __syncthreads(); ``` (No comments at all)

Description: Every note ends with exactly 3 review questions at 3 levels: 1) Recall (definition), 2) Understanding (reasoning), 3) Application (problem-solving).
Example_Good: 1. Recall: What is shared memory? 2. Understanding: Why does shared memory reduce global memory traffic? 3. Application: Design a tiled matrix multiply using shared memory for 32x32 tiles.
Example_Bad: Only 1 question, or all questions at the same difficulty level, or no questions at all.
When_To_Apply: End of every lecture note and atomic concept note

Description: Every note MUST have complete YAML frontmatter with: title, date, course, tags (3+), and aliases (for concept notes). Tags use lowercase-with-hyphens.
Example_Good:
---
title: 'Shared Memory - Fast On-chip Storage'
date: 2026-02-06
course: GPU Computing
tags: [cuda, shared-memory, optimization]
aliases: [共享内存, __shared__, SMEM]
---
Example_Bad: No frontmatter, or incomplete frontmatter missing tags/aliases, or tags with spaces

Description: Use Obsidian [[wiki-links]] for ALL cross-references. Never use standard markdown links for internal notes. Link concept names on first mention.
Example_Good: The [[Roofline Model]] shows whether your kernel is [[Memory-Bound]] or [[Compute-Bound]].
Example_Bad: The Roofline Model shows... (no links) OR [Roofline Model](Roofline-Model.md) (markdown links)

Description: For complex processes, use numbered steps. Each step should build on the previous one. Include intermediate results.
Example_Good: Step 1: Allocate shared memory tile. Step 2: Load data from global memory. Step 3: Synchronize threads. Step 4: Compute using shared memory. Step 5: Synchronize again before next iteration.
Example_Bad: 'Use shared memory by declaring it, loading data, synchronizing, and computing.' (All in one sentence)

Description: Start explanations with a real-world analogy to build intuition before diving into technical details.
Example_Good: 'Think of shared memory like a whiteboard in a meeting room. All people in the room (threads in a block) can read and write to it quickly, but people in other rooms cannot see it.'
Example_Bad: Jumping straight into: 'Shared memory is an on-chip SRAM with configurable size shared among threads in a CTA.'
When_To_Apply: Introduction of new concepts, especially abstract ones

Description: End each concept section with a single-sentence takeaway in a blockquote. This sentence should be flashcard-worthy.
Example_Good: '> **Key Takeaway**: Shared memory reduces global memory traffic by acting as a user-managed cache with ~5 cycle latency.'
Example_Bad: No summary, or a multi-paragraph summary that's too long for a flashcard.

Description: Use hyphens for file names: Concept-Name.md. Use consistent capitalization in titles. Never mix spaces and hyphens in the same project.
Example_Good: Memory-Coalescing.md, Bank-Conflicts.md, Roofline-Model.md
Example_Bad: Memory Coalescing.md, bank-conflicts.md, RooflineModel.md (inconsistent)

Description: Every formula must be accompanied by: 1) variable definitions, 2) intuitive meaning, 3) edge case analysis.
Example_Good: $S = \\frac{1}{s + p/N}$ where s=serial fraction, p=parallel fraction, N=processors. **Meaning**: Even with infinite processors, speedup is limited to 1/s. **Edge case**: If s=0.1, max speedup = 10x.
Example_Bad: $S = 1/(s + p/N)$ (Just the formula with no explanation)

Description: Include a 'Common Pitfalls' section in concept notes listing 2-3 mistakes students typically make, with how to avoid each.
Example_Good: **Pitfall**: Forgetting the second __syncthreads() after computation, causing data races in the next tile iteration. **Fix**: Always pair load-sync-compute-sync.
Example_Bad: No mention of common mistakes

Description: All generated notes go to 00-Inbox/ in the Obsidian vault. User manually moves them to the correct course folder after review.
Example_Good: Output path: ~/...Schaefer_Master/00-Inbox/Shared-Memory.md
Example_Bad: Directly writing to 30-Areas/35-Study/.../GPU Computing/ (bypassing inbox)

Description: When a concept appears in multiple courses, mention the cross-course connection. Use the glossary to find shared terms.
Example_Good: '[[Pipelining]] is also used in FPGA design (see [[FPGA]] course) for increasing throughput.'
Example_Bad: Only mentioning the concept in the current course context
When_To_Apply: When a concept bridges multiple courses

Description: For exam-relevant courses, include hints about common exam question patterns in the review questions section.
Example_Good: '**Exam Pattern**: You may be asked to calculate the speedup using Amdahl''s Law given a serial fraction and number of processors.'
Example_Bad: No exam guidance
When_To_Apply: Exam-relevant courses and review question sections
